content.title: How robust is multi-view consistency under fast camera motion?
content.body: 3D Gaussian Splatting and its SLAM descendants assume reasonably smooth camera trajectories... MonoGS and GS-SLAM report tracking accuracy on Replica/TUM-RGBD but those have well-behaved trajectories. DROID-SLAM is the tracking-accuracy reference on EuRoC/TartanAir where aggressive motion is part of the protocol... 2DGS attacks the *static* version of the property by collapsing primitives to view-consistent disks.

reasoning: I have enough anchors to draw a five-stage timeline. Compose it.
content:
Concept-evolution timeline for 'Multi-View Consistency' across the corpus:

  1. 2020-03 - NeRF (arxiv-2003-08934)
     Establishes photometric multi-view consistency as the implicit optimisation signal. Per-scene loss, no other supervision.

  2. 2021-06 - NeuS (arxiv-2106-10689)
     Lifts the property from 'pixel agreement' to 'view-consistent SDF zero level set'. Geometry is the *thing* that should be consistent, not just radiance.

  3. 2021-08 - DROID-SLAM (arxiv-2108-10869)
     Operationalises the property under aggressive motion: deep bundle adjustment on learned features remains the most robust pose engine on EuRoC / TartanAir.

  4. 2023-08 / 2023-12 - 3DGS (arxiv-2308-04079) and MonoGS (arxiv-2312-06741)
     Splatting takes over view synthesis and SLAM but evaluates only on quasi-static / well-behaved trajectories. The property is assumed, not stress-tested.

  5. 2024-03 - 2DGS (arxiv-2403-17888) and MVSplat (arxiv-2403-14627)
     Two opposing moves on the same gap. 2DGS bakes view-consistency into the primitive geometry (oriented disk). MVSplat raises the question explicitly: feed-forward prediction inherits the consistency gap from sparse views.

Open question: the gap is the fast-motion regime. None of the splatting-SLAM papers report numbers on EuRoC/TartanAir, and no feed-forward GS method has been stress-tested against multi-view-inconsistent inputs. See questions/multi-view-consistency.md.

You're tracing this through GS papers, but 'multi-view consistency' is a term that predates GS by more than a decade — classical MVS used it constantly. Search the pre-NeRF era and the early NeRF era too. I want to see if the splatting authors are using the same definition the MVS people were using.

reasoning: Fair. The corpus has at least DeepV2D (arxiv-1812-04605) and MVS-Texturing-era work from 2016 — let me search for the MVS lineage. There should also be UNISURF (2021) and NeuS (2021) which sit at the implicit-surface / radiance-field boundary.
content: Right — the classical MVS literature owns the term. Broadening the search.

nodes.name: Pixelwise View Selection for Unstructured Multi-View Stereo
DeepV2D: Video to Depth with Differentiable SfM
Implicit Geometric Regularization for Learning Shapes

content.title: Pixelwise View Selection for Unstructured Multi-View Stereo
content.body: Schonberger et al. (the COLMAP MVS paper) define multi-view consistency at the pixel level: a depth hypothesis at pixel p in reference view is consistent if its re-projection into each source view matches the source pixel's intensity AND its depth (forward-backward depth check). The metric is a photometric NCC score over a small window plus a geometric depth-reprojection error in pixels. Inconsistent pixels are filtered out before fusion.

content.title: NeuS: Learning Neural Implicit Surfaces by Volume Rendering for Multi-view Reconstruction
content.body: Wang et al. define multi-view consistency as the property that the recovered SDF's zero level set is identical regardless of which subset of cameras the renderer queries from. They operationalise it via a weighted volume-rendering scheme where the integrand is constructed from the SDF so that the rendered colour is unbiased w.r.t. the surface position. Consistency is enforced by construction (the SDF is shared across views), not by an explicit per-pair check as in COLMAP.

content.title: DeepV2D: Video to Depth with Differentiable Structure from Motion
content.body: Teed and Deng embed classical SfM into a differentiable depth pipeline. Multi-view consistency here is the *photometric* reprojection loss: a depth prediction at frame t is consistent if warping pixels from neighbouring frames into frame t under the predicted depth+pose minimises a robust photometric error. This is the same loss family as classical dense MVS but optimised end-to-end.

reasoning: Three distinct operationalisations are emerging: (1) COLMAP-style photometric+geometric pair check (depth/intensity reprojection), (2) DeepV2D-style differentiable photometric warping loss, (3) NeuS-style 'shared SDF surface' which is by-construction consistency. NeRF sits closer to (2) — the implicit photometric loss across views. 3DGS+MonoGS inherit (2) but never operationalise it as a check; they assume per-scene optimisation will produce it. Let me extend the timeline backward.
content: I have three pre-GS anchors now. COLMAP MVS (2016) operationalises the term explicitly as a per-pair pixel check. DeepV2D (2018) makes that check differentiable as a photometric warping loss. NeuS (2021) reframes it as a geometric property of a shared SDF. That's the lineage the GS papers walked into.
Good. So is there a single definition the corpus agrees on, or are these all incompatible operationalizations of different things people happen to call by the same name?

reasoning: They're not the same. Three genuinely distinct definitions: (a) photometric reprojection consistency (COLMAP, DeepV2D, NeRF photometric loss), (b) geometric reprojection / depth consistency (COLMAP's forward-backward check, DROID-SLAM bundle adjustment residual), (c) surface consistency / shared zero level set (NeuS, UNISURF, 2DGS's view-consistent disk argument). 3DGS optimises (a) implicitly and silently fails on (b) and (c) — that's why SuGaR and 2DGS exist as patches. Let me pull UNISURF to confirm the surface-consistency thread.
content: They're not the same. There are at least three distinct things people call 'multi-view consistency'. Let me confirm against one more paper.

content.title: UNISURF
content.body: Oechsle et al. unify implicit-surface and radiance-field reconstruction by parameterising the occupancy field as the level set of a learned function and rendering it via discrete root-finding inside a volume renderer. Multi-view consistency in UNISURF is identical to NeuS: the recovered occupancy is a single function over space, so any rendering from any subset of cameras refers to the same surface by construction.

facts.subject: Paper:arxiv-1607-02565
facts.object: photometric NCC + geometric forward-backward depth check per pixel pair

facts.subject: Paper:arxiv-2003-08934
facts.object: implicit photometric reprojection loss; no explicit consistency check

facts.subject: Paper:arxiv-2106-10689
facts.object: shared SDF zero level set; consistency by construction

facts.subject: Paper:arxiv-2108-10869
facts.object: deep bundle adjustment residual on learned correlation features

facts.subject: Paper:arxiv-2308-04079
facts.object: photometric loss only; no consistency check; surface not exposed

facts.subject: Paper:arxiv-2403-17888
facts.object: view-consistent surface primitive (oriented disk); consistency by construction at primitive level

reasoning: Now the answer is clean: three operationalisations, no agreement. Final timeline should start at 2016 (COLMAP) and end 2024 (2DGS / MVSplat), grouped by which definition each paper picks up.
content:
No, there's no single corpus-wide definition. Three distinct operationalisations:

  **(1) Photometric reprojection consistency** — the *radiance* at a 3D point matches across all views.
     - COLMAP MVS (2016, arxiv-1607-02565): explicit per-pixel NCC + forward-backward depth check.
     - DeepV2D (2018, arxiv-1812-04605): differentiable photometric warping loss across video frames.
     - NeRF (2020, arxiv-2003-08934): implicit — the per-scene photometric loss IS the consistency objective. No explicit check.
     - 3DGS (2023, arxiv-2308-04079): inherits NeRF's photometric loss; no explicit check; assumed to hold after convergence.

  **(2) Geometric / depth consistency** — the *depth* at a 3D point matches across all views (a stronger property than (1) — radiance can match while depth doesn't, e.g. specular surfaces).
     - COLMAP MVS: forward-backward depth reprojection error in pixels.
     - DROID-SLAM (2021, arxiv-2108-10869): deep bundle adjustment residual on learned correlation features — the production version of (2) under aggressive motion.

  **(3) Surface / topology consistency** — there is a *single shared surface* every view refers to; consistency is by construction, not measured.
     - NeuS (2021, arxiv-2106-10689), UNISURF (2021, arxiv-2104-10078): shared SDF zero level set.
     - 2DGS (2024, arxiv-2403-17888): same idea at primitive level — each primitive is a single oriented disk, the same plane seen from every camera.

The definitions are not equivalent. (3) implies (2) which implies (1); the converses are false. A scene can be photometrically consistent (1) on a specular surface while being geometrically inconsistent (2), because radiance equality doesn't pin down depth. A method that achieves (1) without checking (2) or (3) can produce a multi-view-consistent *render* over a geometrically wrong *scene* — that's the gap the 3DGS-as-SLAM-map papers fall into. The open question (multi-view-consistency under fast motion) is really three questions: which definition matters for which downstream task, and whether per-scene splat optimisation can hit (2) or (3) without explicit supervision.
